channel.title: Game Industry on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Game Industry on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: The Future Of Generative AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the future of generative AI And already I know people’s hackles are rising.

But I’m going to make this a fun Friday because I want to talk about what I think is coming down the pipe. And I wanted to talk about this because, despite the fact that I know many of you hate it’s coming. Tech technology has never been bottled up once it’s been created. So it’s coming. It is going to arrive And I’m going to base what I think is going to show up in both the game industry and beyond on watching technology change in the game industry for the last 45 years and my own PhD work in artificial intelligence in the late 80s and early 90s. So I’ve already talked about artificial intelligence in general and my experience working on it in a video called artificial intelligence, which is linked in the description. I’ve also talked about the effects of artificial intelligence and technology in general on the game industry and other industries where it has actually disrupted them and eliminated them. There are industries that existed when I was a child that don’t exist anymore.

item.title: Carefree To Careful
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the game industry going from carefree to careful.

I’ve been in the industry since 1981. Most of you haven’t even been around since 1981. My career is older than most people who watch this channel And I’ve watched things change. So some of the things I’m going to talk about they’re not new. Um, what instigated this was I was looking through my idea book — This is one of many. The others are on my shelf behind me — And I look through them for ideas of just mechanics, settings, story lines, things I want to work on. And then I talk about them to co-workers.

item.title: Great Developers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about great developers.

I know I’ve told stories on this channel about bad events and bad people And I’ve tried to explain that it’s because those are the learning moments in a lot of my stories. But it’s gotten to the point where some people went: “It sounds like you were surrounded by incompetence” And I’ve tried to explain how that isn’t the tr, isn’t true?

item.title: Fences
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fences.

It’s going to be interesting what I’m talking about because it came out of a comment on another video. That video was the spaghetti code video. I’ll link it below — where I talked about how code can become really not well structured because of frequent changes in the game. A commenter, Derek Posh, and I — he didn’t have a question, We were just talking back and forth about how that happens And I happened to mention that being both a designer and a programmer on the same game still led to spaghetti code, which means even eliminating any communication issues, because obviously programmer Tim knew what designer Tim wanted — still led to spaghetti code. And I talked about how that can happen because design changes And it led me to say that the most eyeopening experiences I’ve had in this industry have come from me being on both sides of a fence. So when I’m talking about fences, I’m not talking about fence sitting, I’m not talking about sitting on a fence and not deciding which one you want to come down on. I’m talking about actually being on both sides of a fence and understanding the issues on both sides and then trying to explain it to people who are firmly planted on one side.

item.title: Arguing About Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arguing about games.

I suppose this might be considered a not so fun Friday video, but I just want to talk about it because it’s something that happens so much. There’s so much arguing. I mean, there’s so much arguing about everything on the on in life, but I make games. So let’s talk about arguing about games. I’ll start by saying people have always argued about games. They’ve always argued about games.

item.title: Game Developer Motivations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game developer motivations.

I see a lot of YouTube videos and game forum posts and even articles on game sites talking about game developers and why they make the games they make and why they do what they do and all kinds of stuff. And most of them are just wildly wrong, often in a very amusing way. When I watch movies that have a programmer usually called a hacker, I laugh at the portrayal because it is usually so funnily amusingly wrong that it’s just it’s funny to watch And of course it makes me go, “Oh, the writer has never actually written a line of code in their life. So this is what they think programmers do and what they’re like and what programmers think. Just by watching programmers”.

item.title: Why Are Modern Games So Big?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

I’m frequently asked: why is it so big? And I’m like, excuse me, Let me back up. I’m frequently asked: why are modern games so much bigger than older games? This comes up a lot on the channel and I’ve answered it many times down in the comments. So I decided to finally make a video about it and talk about it in excruciating detail so that I can just point to people at this video and go, “This is why”. If you’re here for the short answer — why are modern games so big — I’ve got it for you: Art, tap out. Now We’re done. That’s pretty much the whole answer, if you want some nuance to it, if you want some detail on art, and also that other things have gotten bigger, just not as big as art — then that’s what I’m going to talk about for the rest of the video. So let’s dive into it. Let’s start with art, which is the biggest contributor to why the file sizes on a game. That you buy either physically or digitally, is so big.

item.title: Insults and Backhanded Compliments
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about insults and backhanded compliments.

I kind of mean this in a fun way. Not sure this is going to be a fun Friday video or not, You know what. I’ll just let it go up whenever it goes up. But lately I’ve been going through all my notes and I guess what I should call my archive. Now I was looking for a particular thing, but it’s kind of vague, so searching wasn’t helping.

item.title: Why People Quit
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why people quit.

I recently did a video called eating your own dog food — about if you make a product, in particular, a game, you should use it, You should play it, You should eat your own dog food. In the comment section, a lot of people were asking about people who don’t eat their own dog food and, more particularly, why those people would even be in the industry or stay in the game industry if they didn’t play games. And I thought I’d address that in this video, like drill down on it, because I’ve been in the industry.

item.title: Taking Risks
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about taking risks.

I’m going to do this on a fun Friday because it’s not directly about game development, but of course all my stories are game development related because it’s all I’ve ever done. But what I’ve noticed is, especially recently, more and more people are going developers need to take more risks. Why aren’t they taking more risks? A lot of them say it, say it very flippantly, like: come on, just take a risk. Why don’t you? Why, are you guys so cautious?

item.title: "Digital Stealing" And Fairness
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about digital stealing in quotes and fairness.

And I want to explain what I mean by that. But before I do, I want to give some background Love, giving context on these. I’ve made several videos that received a great deal of reaction, strong reaction to the extent that made me rethink my own beliefs. I was basically looking at my own beliefs and going: maybe I shouldn’t think it this way and, you know, was there any hypo hypocritical thinking here? Those videos were my video on capitalism, By the way, I’ll link these all below — And then I did a video on game development caution. I did two parts, part one and two. And then I did kind of two linked videos about, uh, what do gamers want, and do developers even know what gamers want?

item.title: Industry Pendulum Swings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about industry pendulum swings.

Now, what do I mean by that? I’m not talking about Trends. I’m not talking about people going, hey, action RPGs are so hot right now. But I’m also not talking about repeated predictions, like I’m told like every five to seven years that PC gaming is dying again. And it’s not.

What I’m talking about is, over the last four and a half decades I’ve watched things come into the industry and go out of the industry and then come back into the industry and it’s odd to watch these things. And it’s not just game features, it’s Production Way the production’s done, they the way the whole industry is run. So I’m going to talk about some of those, because I kind of want to put some stuff in context so you guys can see that whenever something starts happening in the industry, you have to realize it’s happened before and it’s going to happen again, because these things just go in. And out and in and out, so and out and in and out and in and out. So let me start with one that I first noticed.

item.title: Gaming In The 1980s
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gaming in the 1980s.

Why do I want to talk about this? A whole bunch of reasons, but when I was doing my channel analytics a few weeks ago, I realized my biggest demographic are people between the ages of 25 and 35, which means, because it’s 2025 right now, you were all born in the 1990s, except a little sliver of you that in 2000 — let’s put that in perspective — by the end of the 9s, by 1999, I had a bachelor’s degree in computer science from an engineering school, a master’s degree in computer science specializing in Ai, and I was working on my PhD in aai, specializing in machine learning and, in particular, integrated learning. I’d also been in the game industry for almost 20 years, since 1981, and I had made several games already, including Fallout, and it’s only 1999, which is now 26 years ago. So I wanted to talk about what gaming was like in the 80s. Because it was wildly different than what it is now, and maybe by understanding some of this, or even just knowing it, you can understand some of the trends we see in gaming now. To do this, I’m going to have to probably, give contextual stories, or, for some of you, I’m going to ramble, because you have to understand gaming not only in the context of the ’ 80s, but also in context of all the other entertainment offerings in the80s, and this is going to not only talk about what the times were like, but also the social aspect of a lot of these entertainment choices and how much Focus each one got, how much time people spent in each one of these, because people have so many entertainment options now and you T talk — we’ve talked about this — all the different streaming options and all the different games. Coming out on Steam, there are so many things buying for your attention and buying for your focus that, even though you know steam is putting out, I think it we figured out was like 70 games a day.

item.title: Games And Theater
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about video games and the theater.

This may sound like a weird thing, but I got a bunch of stories to tell. It’ll make sense and there’s even some stuff we can learn from it. But let’s just take a look at the video game business and theater business and look at how, at the very top, they’re very similar. Both are groups of people which are a mix of creatives — you know whether it’s designers and you know artists, vers versus. You know actors and set designers and a lot of technical people, whether it’s programmers or people who do lighting or people who just know how to manage, uh, stage business. Both groups require a lot of discipline and are really helped if they have a good director.

item.title: Learning From Steam Data
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about learning from Steam data.

This is something that I saw — a video that, from my point of view, just came out a couple days ago, but I’m filming this in December, so from your point of view, it’s been a couple months. This video is awesome. It’s linked below. I highly recommend that you watch it. I’m going to repeat that s times because I think it’s super important to watch it. Also, I want to say that people who do videos like this one, that’s actual data and not, you know, someone’s reaction video or someone having the outrage of the week — these are the people I love. So, newbie Indie Game Dev, you’re my new favorite person.

item.title: A Christmas Poem By Ron Gilbert
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do a little Christmas poem by Ron Gilbert.

If you don’t know who Ron Gilbert is, he’s best known for creating scum, which was the script system that was used to create the adventure game Maniac mansion, and from there he went on to create a lot of other awesome adventure games like the secret of Monkey Island, Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade. He went on to do other games to like total Annihilation and death spank and the cave. So you may not know Ron Gilbert. You’ve probably played some of his games and he certainly has been really influential in the game industry.

item.title: Rising Game Budgets
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Rising Game budgets.

This was a question from Irvin Chadwick 5046, who asked: would you be able to give a breakdown on why video games are so much more expensive to make now as compared to 10 years ago? First off, I can compare it to 10 years ago- 20, 30, 40 — and I will — um, I’m also going to assume that this question has nothing to do with sales price. Just to head that off. That’s a completely different discussion of why video games cost more now, or even if they do cost more now, adjusted for inflation, but that’s a whole different discussion. Going to not going to talk about sales price here, I’m going to talk about budget also. This is a general discussion. Notice all the caveats, because I’m just heading off comments.

item.title: Cultural Changes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about cultural changes and I specifically want to get into eventually, because I’m going to tell some stories.

First of why you should be very aware of how fast the culture changes and how this should affect how you design and develop your video games, because you can quickly get stuck with a game that is seems laughable or AIC very quickly, and not because of the graphics but because of your inability to predict cultural changes. But I I will get to that now. I have a whole video about why I don’t like cultural references and I will link it below and just to give you the synopsis right now. I don’t like cultural references for the same reason, I don’t like a lot of humor of a certain kind. I don’t like anything in your game that makes the player feel like not getting it and are being excluded. I don’t like anything that makes the player feel like an other, like, oh shoot, this game is.

item.title: This Channel And The Game Industry
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about this Channel and the game industry, kind of a meta video, a video about my videos, kind of to put in perspective like what

I’ve been trying to do here for a year and a half. Um, and I’ll get to that, but before I get there I I need to tell a couple stories shocker. So My Generation — I think I hate the labels, but I would be the very oldest you can be and still be Gen X — my generation was the one that first really got into computers. I had a computer in high school. I was going online. I was really into technology.

item.title: PAX 2024
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about pxs 2024.

Yes, I went to P here in Seattle over the Labor Day weekend, super fun, and I want to kind of talk about it now. I’ve been to a half a dozen PA conferences before. I’ve been to this one, which sometimes I think it’s called PA West or PAX Prime. I just call it PA. It’s like what I do with Fallout. The first one is just Fallout.

item.title: Capitalism
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about capitalism, but this is going to be a very rambling video and when I start it’s not going to sound like I’m talking about capitalism at all, but I am.

So the other day I was watching a video complaining about the Fallout TV show — not going to tell you which one. There’s a lot of them. People have all kinds of problems with it and I get it and I even agree with some of the problems. I just don’t agree with the degree to which that problem degrades the show. So it’s a matter of taste. Talked about taste a lot, but I started reading the comments in this one and I saw a commenter that said I only like the show because I was paid to attend.

item.title: How The Internet Changed Game Dev
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how did the internet change game development?

This was a question from oonu bogu- 812 — I hope I got that number right. Who said? Who asked: how did the internet change game development in the game industry in general? I remember you saying CD stories about the disc not working and that you made a lot of buggy games. So how would vampire Arcanum be coming out today? Or in what aspects are we better or worse?

item.title: The Business Side Of Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the business side of game development.

This is an answer to a question by Seth, the guy GD, who asks: I was wondering if you could explain the business side of the game industry. I know you started troa. I would love to hear the business stuff about it I know you have a video about troa, but could you go further into the money aspect of it? Could you explain starting up a studio and dealing with hiring people, choosing and approaching Publishers, getting funding for the game, Insurance, banking, PR, office space costs, advertising and other things people wouldn’t think of?

item.title: So You Want To Make Games?
item.description:
It’s me, Tim, so you want to make games today?

I want to answer a question from zaching Tut who asks: I was wondering if you could do a video about things to consider if you’re thinking about getting into the gaming industry, H? Um, this is kind of a big question. Uh, the first thing I want to do is say that I’m to answer this question is really me asking you a bunch of questions. It’s a lot of questions that only you, each individual watching this, can answer. I’m going to break it down into two categories. First, I think you need to ask yourself if you even want to go into the game industry.

item.title: Snapshots Through My Career
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to do something fun that I’m going to call snapshots through my career, and

this is kind of this kind of came up because I’ve done a few videos already: one where I showed scenes of a life in game development, which was a lot of pictures that mostly I took during game development — um, except for all the ones of that people took of me at my desk — and I’ve also done a video on data about my games and I’ll link those below, but they’re not very personal. So what I wanted to do was show you me at every stage of my career — I guess I’d call it like every game I made. So what I did is I went through all the photos — I have thousands of them — and I found a photo of me taken during the development of each one of my games. So I’m going to talk about the game and the year it came out and then show a picture of me at that. Age, so I thought it would be fun. It’s nothing huge, so feel free to skip it, but people seem to like the other ones so I thought I’d do this one.

item.title: Jobs, Careers, And Callings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about having a job versus a career, versus a calling.

Now you may already know what these three different things are, but I’m going to Define them and I also want to talk about how, over the last 40 some years, I’ve watched the proportion of people in each of these three categories change over time in the game industry. Um, it’s something I’ve seen people make comments sometimes going: well, it’s just a job. You know you’re getting taken advantage of at your job, and I want to just point out that some people don’t just view this as a job, and it’s not a small number of people.

item.title: Making A Demo
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about making a demo.

I’ve probably said that phrase in so many other videos, but I’m constantly asked. Even now, it’s been over a year since I’ve been doing this Channel and I’m still asked: hey, Tim, how can I get a job in the industry, despite there being a video titled how to get a go job in the game industry, which I’ll link below. In fact, all the videos I mentioned I’m going to link below, and I always answer this same thing: make a demo. Um, Tim, should I go to school? Make a demo?

item.title: Industry Changes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about industry changes because there have been a lot of stuff going on: a lot of layoffs recently, a lot of concern about AI taking jobs.

People are freaking out and let me just make this clear at the beginning, so no one takes this all out of context later, even though I know some people are. It is bad to lose your job. I’ve lost my job, uh, in the past. It’s bad when you lose your job. That is not what I’m talking about here. I’m talking about news and YouTube, pundits and, you know, industry folks saying the industry is collapsing, it’s collapsing, it’s, horrible.

item.title: "It's A Terrible Time For Games"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

It’s a terrible time for games. I hear this phrase a lot, and not just recently. I’ve always heard this. I’ve heard this since was I? Since I was a kid, when I first got in the industry, when I went back into the industry, throughout my time in the industry and I’m hearing it now.

I’m hearing it’s a bad time for gamers. I’m hearing it’s a bad time for game developers. Bad.

item.title: The Missing R&D Department
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the missing R&D department, and by R&D I mean research and development.

This is a department that I think is missing at most, if not all, game companies, and it’s super important. This is the skunkworks group that really good ideas come out of um. If you’ve watched my Fallout videos, it’s, I think, how Fallout was created: accidentally, because the initial group was an impromptu, random Skunk Works that was thrown together on their own time outside of the normal channel. So by the time production really started, we had a really good idea of what we wanted to make.

item.title: Discoverability
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about discoverability.

Let me tell you what I mean by that. When I started working in the game industry in the 80s, you would be lucky if a new computer role playing game came out once a month. Usually they were only a few a year, so once one came out, you usually had to wait a couple months, maybe even longer, for another one to come out. That was true for almost all the genres, um, except the most simplest, like arcade, like games, where several of those came out a month. Now, compare it to now where, if you included dlc’s and expansions and the mobile market, you could probably play a new RPG every day, and I don’t think I’m exaggerating. So let’s take a look at this from both sides.

item.title: Technological Evolution
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about technological Evolution.

Specifically, I’m going to try to answer a question from bavid deckham 411, who asks: what was your experience with the technological Evolution we’ve gone through during your time making and playing games? Was it ever a source of stress or were you excited to keep up? I’ll give you the tldw: it was exciting and stressful it’s.

item.title: Why Aren't There More Channels Like This?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about why there aren’t more game developers doing channels like this.

I’ve seen a lot of people ask or wonder in comments why there aren’t a lot more channels with people who’ve made games doing this and why I don’t have more people on this channel, and I’m going to break those up. I’m going to talk about why there aren’t channels like this. I’m also going to talk about why I haven’t done more chats because they’re related. And then I want to talk about kind of why it even took me 40 some years to make a to do something like this.

item.title: The Future Of Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the future of games, like where I think games are going to go.

I’ve talked before that there’s a lot of different YouTube channels I like watching and one of them is called like stories of old and I was watching it recently and they were talking about how movies have gone through a bunch of phases, from traditional to Modern to postmodern to meta modern. Um, I don’t pretend to understand all of that. I’m said many times, I’m not the best writer, but my understanding was Modern stories were modern. Movies had stories where the characters were unchanging and had very distinct values, and then the story was them going through an event. Postmodern was kind of tropes version. They like twists, you know it’s oh, it wasn’t who you thought, the killer wasn’t who you thought, or even fourth wall breaking — that happens a lot — where the viewer invited to watch the movie for what it is: a story. And then there’s metamodern, which I won’t claim to understand, but seems to be more of using those elements that postmodern use, like trop aversion or fourth wall breaking, to show you that it’s just a story, to instead tell the story more deeply than It’s Been Told before now.

item.title: Data On My Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to look at data, specifically data for my own games.

I I hear all this discussion about my games — which ones are good, which ones are bad — but today I want to look at hard, factual, objective data, and by objective I mean I can get a number from it. Anybody who goes to look up that number will get the same number. True, you can argue about the numbers, but it’s about as objective as you can get in modern game industry.

item.title: School vs. Self-Taught
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pros and SC cons of going to school versus being self-taught.

If you want to go in the game industry — and remember, this is from my point of view of going to school to learn programming, but this probably has equal measure for Art and Design and other roles that you want to do in games. So let me talk about for a second. Yesterday I talked about a university class that I taught because I I love being in school and I’ve said that I really liked being at UCI, so it was nice to come back and give a little back. Uh, a few weeks before that I talked about how my love for school and all the skills that I think I learned while in school — whether from school or just people — I went to school with the skills that I learned to make games. So this is kind of on top of those.

item.title: It's A Great Time To Be A Game Player
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why this is such an amazing time for game players doesn’t always seem that way, but I’m gonna lay it out.

First of all, there are amazing games coming out. I’m not going to list them because for each one of you it’s different. It’s because there are so many games coming out that there are good games for you. Not every game will be for you, but there are games coming out today are there are games coming out today that child Tim could have only dreamed playing in the 80s?

item.title: The Decline Of Generalists
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the decline of generalists, and I’ll describe what that is.

But I’ve gotten a lot of questions from people who say what’s changed the most in development over the last 40 years. And I’ve thought about this a lot because there’s so much that’s changed. I mean the nature of games have changed, the Hardware’s changed, the structure of companies have changed, the money involved has massively changed, the diversity in the industry has changed, the just pretty much everything about the industry is wildly different. So this is something that’s very peculiar and specific to the game industry that a lot of people outside the game industry would probably have no ideas even happening. So I’ll have to talk about what I mean by generalist — why it’s, why they’re in Decline and the problems I think that introduces. So first of all, what do I mean by generalist, a generalist is someone who’s skilled in multiple disciplines.

item.title: Virtual Reality
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim. Can we talk about VR?

Oh, hold on, okay, that’s a lot better. Yeah, let’s talk about VR. I have been doing VR — not development, but just keeping an eye on it, buying different pieces of Hardware, trying it out — for probably at Le, at least the last 5 years, if not more. I’ve had this particular helmet. It’s the — uh, Oculus meta Quest 2. I don’t remember what its name is and I’ll get into that in a second.

item.title: Career Summary 1981-2023
item.description: Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer a question from asdfjk1227 who asks: can you do a resume video where you talk about every gaming project you did and then a couple, two short versions of interesting events or something, so we can get a better understanding of your experiences in one spot, like 67, 60 seconds per project or company maximum to get a high level?

item.title: Game Development: Then vs. Now
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to ask a question from, or answer a question — I keep doing that from — Jeremy Mason 9802 asks: do you think game development is harder or Worse nowadays compared to, say, the 90s, or is it better or the same?

Here’s how I’m going to answer that, because I think that is a very nuanced question that needs a nuanced answer. So here’s what I’m going to do: I’m going to tell you some things that I think are better, I’m going to tell you some things that I think are worse, and I’m going to tell you some things that go either way, mainly because, depending on you, may think it’s a better thing, you may think it’s a worse thing or you may not even care about that thing. So let me tell you, let me start with what I think is better, and also, I think it’s funny that you said the 90s, since I started in 1981, the Whole Decade there. Uh, the 80s, where it was.

item.title: Career Longevity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about career longevity.

I’ve had several questions from people, and not just on the channel. Um, people have asked me this before: how did you last so long in the game industry? And some of these questions have come from people who tried it, who’ve been in the industry and made it two years, five years, 10 years, and then Tapped Out. And I’ve said it once and I say it again: making games is hard. People think it’s fun and you know, uh, you know a silly, you know industry, you know, and oh, there’s a lot harder jobs than that, true, but it’s still a hard job. I think there’s a number of ways I can answer this question. Maybe any one of them’s right, but taken all together, I think it’s why I stayed in the business so long.

item.title: Work-Life Balance
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my thoughts on work-life balance in the game industry.

This is something that’s getting a lot of attention recently. A lot of people are talking about this, and I agree it’s a problem, so I’m just gonna — I’m gonna start and end this video saying this: work-life balance is important. I’ve noticed some people will watch my videos and walk away with very unusual takes on what I said, so I’m gonna make that clear. I started and stopped this video multiple times, and I’ve never done that. I usually sit down, think about what I want to say, do it, maybe bring up some notes to make sure I stay focused, but. This one had me starting and stopping and I finally realized what it was.

item.title: Ageism In The Game Industry
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about ageism in the game industry and that’s why I’m wearing a shirt which you may have noticed, is a little tight.

This is the very first shirt I ever received. You can see it’s from Pegasus software. I got this shirt 40 years ago. I am the same height — six one — as I was when I received this shirt, when I was, I think, 18.. I’m not the same weight. I was 135 pounds there then. I am not 135 pounds now, but you can see it’s a little tight, but I’m gonna wear it.

item.title: How to Get a Job in the Game Industry
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to answer the most asked question I ever get, not just on this channel but at conventions, by email when people visit the studios I worked at friends, family.

The number one asked question: how do I get a job in the game industry? Often with a sub question: how did you get your job? Implying that I’ll just do that. I’m going to answer the second question first because it’s fun. Then I’m going to answer the first question with tough love. Some of you won’t like the answer. Okay, so the first question or the second question I’m gonna, which I answer first, is: how did I get a job? Kind of goes back to wow, 1976 I think.
